title: Agents of Chaos Duology
content:
{{Top|ffa|rwm|njo|title=''Agents of Chaos'' Duology}}
{{BookSeries
|image=[[File:Agentsofchaoshc.jpg]]
|title=''Agents of Chaos'' Duology
|author=[[James Luceno]]<ref name="ht">''[[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial]]''</ref><ref name="je">''[[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse]]''</ref>
|cover artist=*[[Rick Berry]] (American)<ref name="ht" /><ref name="je" />
*[[Tsuyoshi Nagano]] (Japanese)<ref name="jap1">[[:File:Herostrialjc.jpg|''Hero's Trial'' Japanese cover]]</ref><ref name="jap2">[[:File:Jedieclipsejc.jpg|''Jedi Eclipse'' Japanese cover]]</ref>
|illustrator=

|first date=[[August 11]], [[2000]] ([[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial|''Hero's Trial'']])<ref name="proper ht date">{{WebCite|url=http://www.starwarsmedia.com/_heros_trial.html|text=Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial|work=Starwarsmedia.com|archivedate=20160729204253}}</ref>
|last date=[[October]] 2000 ([[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse|''Jedi Eclipse'']])<ref name="jeproper date">{{WebCite|url=http://www.starwarsmedia.com/_jedi_eclipse.html|text=Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse|work=Starwarsmedia.com|archivedate=20160729204308}}</ref>
|media type=Novels<ref name="ht" /><ref name="je" />

|preceded by=[[Dark Tide duology|''Dark Tide'' duology]]<ref name="Onslaught">''[[The New Jedi Order: Dark Tide I: Onslaught]]''</ref><ref name="Ruin">''[[The New Jedi Order: Dark Tide II: Ruin]]''</ref>
|followed by=''[[The New Jedi Order: Balance Point]]''<ref name="Balance Point">''[[The New Jedi Order: Balance Point]]''</ref>
}}
{{Quote|I felt bad about not being able to do more with Han Solo in my novels, but the two books that follow mine focus on him, so my job was help him to mark time until Jim Luceno can roar back with a pair of Han Solo novels. I can't wait for them.|Michael A. Stackpole|<ref name="Stackpole JC interview">{{WebCite|url=http://theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/stackpole3a.asp|text=Jedi Council – Interviews – Michael A. Stackpole – part 1 of 3|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20190730114125}}</ref>}}
The '''''Agents of Chaos'' Duology''' is a pair of novels that were written by [[James Luceno]] in the year [[2000]]. ''[[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial]]'' and ''[[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse]]'' are respectively the fourth and fifth volumes of the multi-author book series known as [[Star Wars: The New Jedi Order|''The New Jedi Order'']], and they cover the latter half of the [[25 ABY/Legends|first year]] of the [[Yuuzhan Vong War]], an invasion of [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]] by an [[Extragalactic/Legends|extra-galactic]] [[Species/Legends|species]] of [[True Way|religious]] zealots known as the [[Yuuzhan Vong]]. As the [[New Republic/Legends|New Republic]] battles back against the [[Blood/Legends|bloodthirsty]] invaders, its former [[Chief of State (New Republic)/Legends|Chief of State]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa Solo]] works to relocate [[Refugee/Legends|refugees]] who have been displaced by the war. Concurrently, members of the Yuuzhan Vong [[Priest caste]] plot to destroy [[Jedi Master/Legends|Master]] [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker's]] [[New Jedi Order|Jedi Order]], and the [[Pilot/Legends|pilot]] [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]], stricken with grief over the [[Death/Legends|death]] of his long-time friend and co-pilot [[Chewbacca/Legends|Chewbacca]], travels across the [[Galaxy/Legends|galaxy]] to help both old and new friends in their [[Time/Legends|times]] of need, hoping to find a way to come to terms with his loss.

James Luceno was originally hired as a consultant and [[Canon|continuity]] advisor for ''The New Jedi Order'', but he was eventually contracted to write a single novel called ''Agents of Chaos''. However, when the preceding [[Dark Tide duology|''Dark Tide'' duology]] by [[Michael A. Stackpole]] was reduced to a duology, ''Agents of Chaos'' was expanded into two books. Luceno wrote the duology as a tribute to his late friend [[Brian Daley]], who had penned a ''[[Star Wars: The Han Solo Adventures]]'' of Han Solo–centric novels over twenty years prior. Luceno intended for ''Hero's Trial'' to be a "Han Solo, this is your life" novel, and he also hoped to take Solo through a heroic journey that recalled the [[Original trilogy|original ''Star Wars'' trilogy]]. The two volumes of ''Agents of Chaos'' were released in paperback, a hardcover collecting both novels published by the [[Science Fiction Book Club]], and audio cassette in [[August]] and [[October]] 2000, respectively, and although reception was mixed, several characters and other elements introduced in the duology played important roles throughout the remainder of ''The New Jedi Order''. Luceno later went on to write the series' finale, [[2003]]'s ''[[The New Jedi Order: The Unifying Force]]''.

==Plot summary==
===''Hero's Trial''===
{{Main|The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial}}
{{Quote|I'll deal with this in my own way.|Han Solo, on Chewbacca's death|<ref name="ht" />}}
[[25 ABY/Legends|Twenty-one years]] after the [[Battle of Endor/Legends|Battle of Endor]] and the [[Death/Legends|death]] of [[Galactic Emperor/Legends|Galactic Emperor]] [[Palpatine/Legends|Palpatine]], [[The galaxy/Legends|the galaxy]] has been [[Yuuzhan Vong War|invaded]] by an [[Extragalactic/Legends|extra-galactic]] [[Species/Legends|species]] of [[True Way|religious]] zealots known as the [[Yuuzhan Vong]]. Both the [[New Republic/Legends|New Republic]] and [[Jedi Master/Legends|Master]] [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker's]] [[New Jedi Order]] have been attempting to repel the [[Blood/Legends|bloodthirsty]] invaders, but [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]], Skywalker's brother-in-law, has slipped into [[Alcohol/Legends|alcoholism]] and depression following the [[Destruction of Sernpidal|death]] of his long-time friend and co-[[Pilot/Legends|pilot]] [[Chewbacca/Legends|Chewbacca]].<ref name="Onslaught" /><ref name="Ruin" /><ref name="Vector Prime">''[[The New Jedi Order: Vector Prime]]''</ref> After attending a [[Funeral of Chewbacca|funeral]] for the fallen [[Soldier/Legends|warrior]], he pushes his [[Solo family/Legends|family]] away and vows to avenge his friend's death without their help. He is soon greeted by his old friend [[Roa]], who informs him that a former acquaintance of theirs, [[Reck Desh]], has joined a group called the [[Peace Brigade]] and is cooperating with the Yuuzhan Vong. Actions taken by the Peace Brigade resulted in the death of Roa's [[Marriage/Legends|wife]] [[Lwyll]], and he asks for Solo's help in tracking down Desh and preventing the organization from costing any more innocent lives. Solo agrees, and leaves the [[Galaxy/Legends|galactic]] [[Capital/Legends|capital]] of [[Coruscant/Legends|Coruscant]] without informing his family where he is going.<ref name="ht" />

As the Yuuzhan Vong continue to conquer [[Planet/Legends|planets]] along their invasion corridor, the [[Priest caste|priest]] [[Harrar]] learns of the [[Jedi/Legends|Jedi]] and [[The Force/Legends|the Force]] and decides that the invasion is a holy war between the Yuuzhan Vong's [[Yun'o|True Gods]] and the "False [[Deity/Legends|God]]" of the Force. He and the [[Executor (Yuuzhan Vong rank)|Executor]] [[Nom Anor]] devise a plan to turn the [[Priest/Legends|priestess]] [[Elan]] over to the New Republic as a bogus defector, arming her with [[Poison/Legends|poisonous]] and deadly [[bo'tous spore]]s. Elan and her [[familiar]], the [[Fosh/Legends|Fosh]] Vergere, are delivered to the New Republic and are debriefed by [[New Republic Intelligence Service|New Republic Intelligence]].<ref name="ht" />

Solo and Roa travel to [[Ord Mantell/Legends|Ord Mantell]] in Roa's [[Starship/Legends|ship]], the ''[[Happy Dagger]]'', to the [[Orbit/Legends|orbiting]] [[Space station/Legends|space station]] known as the [[Jubilee Wheel (space station)|''Jubilee Wheel'']]. There, they are provided with information on Desh from [[Big Bunji/Legends|Big Bunji]], the ''Jubilee Wheel''{{'s}} owner and an old associate of Solo's, as well as from the young Human [[Fasgo]]. The Yuuzhan Vong soon arrive in-[[Bright Jewel system/Legends|system]], however, and [[Battle of Ord Mantell (Yuuzhan Vong War)|attack]] Ord Mantell. The New Republic is quick to respond, having been tipped off by Elan. Anor and Harrar intend for the [[Space warfare/Legends|attack]] to fail in an effort to further their ruse, but they yet use the opportunity to gather [[Slavery/Legends|slaves]] by attacking the ''Wheel'' with a [[qworlth skaal]] creature, which begins ripping away the station's exterior bulkheads and inhaling the passengers into its gaping [[Mouth/Legends|mouth]]. Roa and Fasgo are pulled into the maw, but Solo holds steadfastly onto a torn bulkhead. He then teams up with a member of the [[Ryn/Legends|Ryn]] species to close the corridor's [[Blast door/Legends|blast shield]].<ref name="ht" />

[[File:JublileeWheel_njosb.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The ''Jubilee Wheel'' is attacked by a qworlth skaal.]]
The Ryn saves Solo's life in the process, and their partnership continues as they lead a group of [[Refugee/Legends|refugees]] to a [[Shuttle/Legends|shuttle]] in the station's [[Hangar/Legends|launching bays]]. They pilot it down to the surface of Ord Mantell before parting ways, and Solo runs into his wife, [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa Solo]], who has been sent to Ord Mantell by the [[New Republic Senate/Legends|New Republic Senate]] to help care for refugees displaced by the invaders. He refuses to return to Coruscant with her, and then gets tipped off by [[Big Bunji's Aqualish lieutenant|Bunji's lieutenant]] that Reck Desh has an operation planned in the [[Bilbringi system/Legends|Bilbringi system]]. He arranges passage there on the [[Luxury liner/Legends|luxury liner]] ''[[Queen of Empire]]'', where he again encounters the Ryn, who introduces himself as [[Droma]] and shares Solo's quarters for the night.<ref name="ht" />

In an attempt to covertly bring the defectors to Coruscant, [[Major/Legends|Major]] [[Showolter]] of New Republic Intelligence boards the luxury liner with Elan and Vergere, but is attacked by members of the Peace Brigade who hope to return the defectors to the Yuuzhan Vong. Injured, Showolter spots Solo and assumes that he has been sent by the New Republic. He turns the defectors over to Solo and Droma while he heads to [[Medical bay/Legends|sick bay]]. Nom Anor, meanwhile, brings a [[Fleet/Legends|fleet]] to Bilbringi in order to co-opt the bothersome actions of the ignorant Peace Brigade, and stages a [[Battle of Bilbringi (Yuuzhan Vong War)|mock attack]] on the ''Queen of Empire''. As [[New Republic Defense Fleet/Legends|forces]] of the New Republic arrive, Organa Solo brings Solo's [[YT-1300 light freighter/Legends|ship]], the [[Millennium Falcon/Legends|''Millennium Falcon'']], to the ''Queen of Empire'', where it is quickly commandeered by her husband after he loses the defectors to Desh. Solo and Droma find their quarry's shuttle drifting in [[Realspace/Legends|space]], and Solo boards it to discover everyone save for the defectors lying dead.<ref name="ht" />

After he brings Elan and Vergere aboard the ''Millennium Falcon'', Solo realizes that the Yuuzhan Vong [[Warship/Legends|warship]] firing on them is deliberately missing, and suspects a trap. He confronts Elan while Droma takes the ship's controls, and the priestess, realizing that her deception has been discovered, releases the poisonous spores into the air, committing suicide by swallowing some. Solo's life is saved when Droma abandons his post on a hunch and opens a door to the rear hold, providing Solo with some air. Vergere flees in an [[Escape pod/Legends|escape pod]] after giving Solo a vial of her tears, and Anor's warship is chased out of the [[Star system/Legends|system]]. Back on Coruscant, Master Skywalker uses the tears to help control a Yuuzhan Vong [[Coomb spore|illness]] contracted by his wife [[Mara Jade Skywalker|Mara Jade]], and Solo, grateful for Droma having saved his life several times, vows to help the Ryn find his family members who have been displaced by the Yuuzhan Vong. With their plan having failed, Harrar and Nom Anor are berated by the Yuuzhan Vong [[Supreme Commander (Yuuzhan Vong rank)|Supreme Commander]] [[Nas Choka]] as they prepare to redeploy their fleet to [[Hutt Space/Legends|Hutt Space]] and ally with the [[Hutt/Legends|Hutts]].<ref name="ht" />

===''Jedi Eclipse''===
{{Main|The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse}}
{{Quote|We are not simply standing by, waiting for the Yuuzhan Vong to strike.|New Republic Admiral Sien Sovv|<ref name="je" />}}
One [[Standard month/Legends|month]] [[Time/Legends|later]], Organa Solo is on [[Gyndine/Legends|Gyndine]] evacuating refugees as the Yuuzhan Vong [[Battle of Gyndine|attack]] the planet. The [[Jedi Knight/Legends|Jedi Knight]] [[Wurth Skidder]] is fighting on the front lines and intentionally remains on-world, letting himself be captured by the invaders so that he might learn more about their war-coordinating [[yammosk]] creatures. On Coruscant, several high-ranking [[Officer/Legends|officers]] of the New Republic military learn of Gyndine's fall and begin making plans to halt the invading war machine. They convince Organa Solo to travel to the [[Hapes Cluster/Legends|Hapes Cluster]] and request assistance from the ruling [[Hapes Consortium/Legends|Hapes Consortium]], and devise a strategy to lure the Yuuzhan Vong to the [[Corellian system/Legends|Corellian system]] where the ancient [[Superweapon/Legends|superweapon]] [[Centerpoint Station]], once operational, can use its destructive power to devastate an attacking Yuuzhan Vong fleet.<ref name="je" />

Nom Anor and [[Commander/Legends|Commander]] [[Malik Carr]] of the Yuuzhan Vong travel to Hutt Space and ally with the ruling Hutt [[Kajidic/Legends|kajidics]], hoping to manipulate them into passing false intelligence to the New Republic. The greedy Hutts do just that, spreading news of the Yuuzhan Vong's supposed upcoming targets to the [[Information broker/Legends|information broker]] [[Talon Karrde]], who shares it with both New Republic Intelligence and with the New Jedi Order. As an old friend of Skywalker's, he hopes that the Jedi can use the information to keep their relationship with the New Republic strong.<ref name="je" />

In the newly-painted matte-[[Color/Legends|black]] ''Millennium Falcon'', Solo and Droma begin searching for Droma's family while hoping to uncover information on Roa's whereabouts in the process. On [[Tholatin]], they learn that a contractor is paying freelancers to transport refugees to planets that the Yuuzhan Vong plan to attack, and that a group of refugees is soon due to be transported off of [[Ruan]]. They arrive on Ruan too late to stop Droma's clanmates from fleeing with the freelancers, and Droma, mistaken for one of his family members, is arrested for forgery of official documents. He is forced into labor at a [[product enhancement facility]], but is quickly rescued by Solo. The two flee in a [[Landspeeder/Legends|landspeeder]], but [[Bow (Human)|Bow]], a sentry at the facility, manipulates [[Weather control/Legends|weather control]] and sends a [[Water/Legends|tidal wave]] after them. They are picked up by a [[87-RM Scout Collector|Scout Collector]] controlled by the [[BFL]]-series [[Droid/Legends|droid]] [[Baffle]], who has befriended Solo, and who tells them of a refugee ship headed for [[Fondor/Legends|Fondor]].<ref name="je" />

Organa Solo travels to the world of [[Hapes/Legends|Hapes]] and appeals to the [[Hapan]] [[Queen Mother]], [[Teneniel Djo]], to aid the New Republic. The [[Queen/Legends|Queen]] Mother's husband, [[Prince/Legends|Prince]] [[Isolder]], sways the Consortium's vote in Organa Solo's favor, but she receives a [[Force vision/Legends|Force vision]] predicting disaster and too late warns Isolder to reconsider. Her son [[Anakin Solo|Anakin]], meanwhile, has been recruited by the [[New Republic Defense Force/Legends|New Republic military]] to help reactivate Centerpoint Station, seven [[Standard year/Legends|years]] after imparting his imprint on the station's firing controls.<ref name="je" />

The refugees left behind on Gyndine are brought aboard the Yuuzhan Vong [[clustership]] [[Crèche (clustership)|''Crèche'']] and enslaved, forced to tend to a developing yammosk. Skidder encounters Roa, Fasgo, and Droma's sister [[Sapha]], and begins [[Telepathy/Legends|telepathically]] conversing with the yammosk. He attempts to convince it that the Hutts will betray the Yuuzhan Vong, but the yammosk reacts angrily and kills Fasgo. [[Chine-kal]], the commander of the vessel, discovers that Skidder is a Jedi and prepares to offer him to the Yuuzhan Vong [[Warmaster]] [[Tsavong Lah]]. He begins to slowly [[Torture/Legends|torture]] the Jedi.<ref name="je" />

[[File:BattleofFondor.jpg|thumb|left|175px|The Hapan fleet is devastated at Fondor.]]
Karrde identifies [[Tynna/Legends|Tynna]], [[Bothawui/Legends|Bothawui]], and [[Corellia/Legends|Corellia]] as upcoming targets of the Yuuzhan Vong, and when Tynna [[Battle of Tynna|falls]], [[Commodore/Legends|Commodore]] [[Turk Brand|Brand]] of the New Republic military manipulates the Senate's [[New Republic Advisory Council|Advisory Council]] into voting to withdraw ships to Bothawui so that Corellia may remain open for attack. Although they are unaware of Centerpoint, Carr and Anor capture Tynna in an effort to mislead the New Republic, actually intending to strike at the [[Fondor Shipyards/Legends|shipyards of Fondor]]. They do not, however, anticipate the Hutts spreading information that ''Crèche'' is headed for [[Kalarba/Legends|Kalarba]] with a Jedi Knight aboard. Karrde informs the Jedi Knights [[Kyp Durron]] and [[Ganner Rhysode]], who bring Durron's [[Starfighter/Legends|starfighter]] [[Squadron/Legends|squadron]], [[Kyp's Dozen]], to Kalarba and then chase ''Crèche'' to Fondor. The Dozen arrives in the [[Fondor system/Legends|Fondor system]] at the same time as the ''Millennium Falcon'' and a Yuuzhan Vong armada, which is forced to strike earlier than anticipated and begins attacking the [[New Republic First Fleet|New Republic First Battle Group]]. Durron assigns two of his craft to aid the ''Millennium Falcon'' in pursuing the [[Trevee|refugee ship]] they are chasing, and the outgunned freelancers agree to turn their [[Prisoner/Legends|prisoners]] over to Droma and Solo. Durron and Rhysode board ''Crèche'', and although a weak and exhausted Skidder dies before they can rescue him, Rhysode kills the yammosk, and the two Jedi bring the [[Prison/Legends|prisoners]] aboard the ''Millennium Falcon''.<ref name="je" />

The Hapes Consortium sends over 100 of their [[Capital ship/Legends|capital ships]] to aid the New Republic, and join Brand's [[Flagship/Legends|flagship]], the ''[[Yald]]'', in traveling to [[Fondorian]] space after learning of the attack. Anakin's brother [[Darth Caedus|Jacen]] is heavily against the re-enabling of Centerpoint, and goes with Anakin to the station. Within, they find their [[Parent/Legends|father's]] cousin [[Thrackan Sal-Solo]], who has joined the effort to get the station operational. Anakin is able to reactivate Centerpoint's systems, but, influenced by Jacen, decides not to fire the weapon. Sal-Solo seizes the controls and fires the station's powerful [[Repulsorlift/Legends|repulsor]] weapon into Fondorian space, but, without the accuracy that Anakin would have commanded, destroys three quarters of the Hapan fleet in addition to half of the Yuuzhan Vong armada. Anor and Carr are shocked and retreat, and the severely weakened Hapans abandon the war to return to their consortium. Nom Anor discovers that the New Republic [[Senator/Legends|Senator]] Viqi Shesh attempted to warn the Yuuzhan Vong about Centerpoint, and travels to Coruscant in [[Ooglith masquer|disguise]] to establish a working relationship with her.<ref name="je" />

==Development==
===Conception===
{{Quote|His credentials are impeccable.|Author [[R. A. Salvatore]], on James Luceno joining the writing team of ''The New Jedi Order''|<ref name="TFN Salvatore Interview 2">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/jedicouncil/interview/salvatore2.shtml|text=Cellblock 1138: R.A. Salvatore – Part 2|author=Keier, Helen|work=[[TheForce.net]]|archivedate=20000302114055}}</ref>}}
[[File:JamesLuceno.jpg|thumb|left|150px|James Luceno]]
In the late 1970s, [[James Luceno]] was traveling the world with his friend [[Brian Daley]], who was plotting the storyline of [[Star Wars: The Han Solo Adventures|''The Han Solo Adventures'']], three ''[[Star Wars]]'' novels that he was writing for [[Del Rey]] Books. Daley used Luceno as a sounding board while crafting the plot,<ref name="terrania">{{WebCite|url=http://terrania.us/libnotes/2007/02/episode-15-james-luceno-interview.html|text=Episode 15 - James Luceno Interview (Part 2)|work=Space Station Liberty|archiveurl=https://archive.is/9vsQo|nolive=1}}</ref> and later consulted him again when writing the [[Star Wars radio dramas|radio dramatizations]] of the [[Original trilogy|original ''Star Wars'' trilogy]].<ref name="eucantinaluceno">{{WebCite|url=http://test.eucantina.net/interview-with-author-james-luceno|text=Interview with Author James Luceno|work=EUCantina.net|archivedate=20150401081302|nolive=1}}</ref> Luceno's official involvement in the ''Star Wars'' [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]] began in the late 1990s when he was hired by Del Rey and [[Lucasfilm]] as a consultant during the planning of [[Star Wars: The New Jedi Order|''The New Jedi Order'']] book series.<ref name="Wrapping Up">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/books/unjoh/interviews/lucenotos.html|text=Wrapping It All Up – An Interview with James Luceno|work=[[TheForce.net|The Unofficial NJO Homepage]]|archivedate=20200201043532}}</ref> ''The New Jedi Order'' was a multi-author series that was going to take several years to publish,<ref name="Novel Approach">{{InsiderCite|66|Novel Approach}}</ref> and Luceno was brought in to help coordinate the project due to his experience overseeing and co-writing a multibook saga in the [[Wikipedia:Robotech|''Robotech'']] universe.<ref name="Wrapping Up" />

Luceno attended several planning meetings for the series at [[Skywalker Ranch]], and joined a [[Canon|continuity]] team<ref name="Wrapping Up" /> that also included author [[Daniel Wallace|Dan Wallace]].<ref name="tyers23">{{JCFCite|url=posts/1316251|board=Literature|thread=Discuss BP with Kathy -- Spoilers Welcome|user=[[Kathy Tyers|Shmi53]]|date=2000-11-16|time=8:14 AM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200312121229/https://boards.theforce.net/threads/discuss-bp-with-kathy-spoilers-welcome.1224749/page-28}}</ref> The two expanded a map of the ''Star Wars'' galaxy, and Luceno wrote a "series bible" to coordinate all of the story arcs. He also helped draft and refine the original outline of the series<ref name="TUF interview">{{SWArchive|url=eu/lit/novel/f20031104/index.html|text=NJO Finale: ''The Unifying Force''}}</ref> that was submitted to [[George Lucas]], the creator of ''Star Wars'';<ref name="Fictional Frontiers">{{WebCite|url=http://www.fictionalfrontiers.podcastpeople.com/posts/27756|text=Interview with James Luceno (Unedited)|work=Fictional Frontiers with Sohaib|archivedate=20170914190136|nolive=1}}</ref> Luceno additionally read and commented on the manuscripts submitted by ''The New Jedi Order''{{'s}} various authors.<ref name="Wrapping Up" /> It was initially undecided if he would be contributing any books to the series,<ref name="Fictional Frontiers" /> but he was eventually contracted to write a single novel entitled ''Agents of Chaos'' in [[2000]], which would be the fifth book of the series. It was set to take place after [[Michael A. Stackpole]]'s [[Dark Tide duology|''Dark Tide'' Trilogy]],<ref name="100 lessons">{{InsiderCite|74|The New Jedi Order in 100 Easy Lessons}}</ref> but changes made to ''The New Jedi Order''{{'s}} storyline necessitated that ''Dark Tide'' be compressed into a duology.<ref name="Stackpole unjoh">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/books/unjoh/interviews/index.html#stack1|text=Mike Stackpole Interview|work=[[TheForce.net|The Unofficial NJO Homepage]]|archivedate=20190730225123}}</ref> ''Agents of Chaos'' was thus expanded to two books:<ref name="100 lessons" /> [[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial|''Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial'']] and [[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse|''Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse'']].<ref name="ht" /><ref name="je" /> Their working titles were ''Solo Crusade'' and ''The Crooked Sky''.<ref name="Reader's Companion">''[[The Essential Reader's Companion]]''</ref>

Luceno's duology was to focus heavily on the character of Han Solo,<ref name="terrania" /> who had first appeared in the [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|original ''Star Wars'' film]] in [[1977]].<ref name="A New Hope">{{Film|IV}}</ref> Solo had played a major role in ''Star Wars'' novels published by [[Bantam Spectra]] throughout the 1990s, but [[Shelly Shapiro]], Del Rey Editor and one of the primary planners of ''The New Jedi Order'', felt that many of Bantam's authors had not known what to do with him.<ref name="Novel Approach" /> In ''Dark Tide'', Stackpole wrote Solo as slipping into depression and alcoholism after the death of Chewbacca,<ref name="Onslaught" /><ref name="ruin">''[[The New Jedi Order: Dark Tide II: Ruin]]''</ref> and he regretted not being able to do more with the character. However, he took comfort in the fact that Luceno was due to write a pair of Solo-centric novels immediately thereafter.<ref name="Stackpole JC interview" />

===Production===
{{Quote|Nice handoff, Bob; here's the baton for you, Jim.|Michael A. Stackpole passes writing duties for ''The New Jedi Order'' to James Luceno|<ref name="Ruin" />}}
[[File:Daley_Luceno_Barbieri.jpg|thumb|right|275px|Luceno (center left) wrote the Agents of Chaos duology as a tribute to his deceased friend Brian Daley (left).]]
Having been directly involved in creating the storyline of the series, Luceno did not find it difficult to step into a writing role. He did, however, spend a good deal of time learning the rules and regulations of the ''Star Wars'' franchise,<ref name="terrania" /> even after having read many of the Bantam-issued novels in the 1990s.<ref name="eucantinaluceno" /> He saw the opportunity to focus on Solo as a fortuitous one, as it allowed him to pay homage to Brian Daley, who had passed away in [[1996]].<ref name="terrania" /> Feeling that Daley had captured Solo's character excellently in ''The Han Solo Adventures'', Luceno was able to pay tribute to his friend. Citing Daley as a mentor of his, Luceno claimed to have felt his friend's presence on his shoulder while writing the ''Agents of Chaos'' Duology, and was glad for the opportunity to honor him.<ref name="serving Sidious">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/books/unjoh/interviews/index.html#luceno|text=Serving Darth Sidious: Author James Luceno Delves into Dark Minds|work=[[TheForce.net|The Unofficial NJO Homepage]]|archivedate=20161220031933}}</ref><ref name="tosagents">{{SWArchive|url=eu/lit/novel/f20001023/index.html|text=Agent of Chaos: An Interview with James Luceno}}</ref>

Luceno's eagerness to write about Solo also stemmed from his hope that he could take the character through a heroic journey reminiscent of the original ''Star Wars'' trilogy. He did find sweet-and-sour irony in his writing subject, however, having shared a friendship with Daley that he saw as akin to Solo's and Chewbacca's for twenty-five years.<ref name="tosagents" /> His aim was to approach Solo's grief in a very adult way, and to push the character away from his wife so that he could find himself.<ref name="Fictional Frontiers" /> Luceno found writing Solo as older, isolated, and grief-stricken to be a challenge,<ref name="serving Sidious" /> but he has identified Solo as one of his favorite characters to write, citing the humanness of the character and the fact that he has not been able to grow and evolve in the same way as his [[Force-sensitive/Legends|Force-using]] family.<ref name="eucantinaluceno" /> ''Hero's Trial'', to be a "Han Solo, this is your life" novel.<ref name="serving Sidious" /> Several characters from Daley's novels reappear within the pages of the book.<ref name="ht" /> Many of the characters in the duology are of Luceno's own creation, however, including Droma,<ref name="terrania" /> who becomes Chewbacca's replacement as the co-pilot of the ''Millennium Falcon'' in ''Jedi Eclipse''.<ref name="je" /> Luceno also created Droma's species, the Ryn, and modeled them after the [[Wikipedia:Romani people|Romany people]].<ref name="terrania" /><ref name="tosagents" />

Big Bunji's role in ''Hero's Trial'' was initially going to be filled by the character of [[Boba Fett/Legends|Boba Fett]], but Fett was written out of the story due to concerns that too many characters were being added. Roa was originally going to die during the attack on the ''Jubilee Wheel'', but his fate was altered as Luceno further developed the story. The refugee plotline that runs through the ''Agents of Chaos'' Duology was part of ''The New Jedi Order''{{'s}} initial story outline, designed to give a point of view to the galaxy's lowest classes, echoing the role played by C-3PO and [[R2-D2/Legends|R2-D2]] in the original ''Star Wars'' film.<ref name="Reader's Companion" />

''Hero's Trial'' was released in paperback and audio cassette<ref name="htamazon">{{RandomHouseOld|url=audio/catalog/display.pperl?isbn=0375415734|text=Star Wars: Agents of Chaos: Hero's Trial}}</ref> in [[August]] 2000.<ref name="proper ht date" /> ''Jedi Eclipse'' was released in the same formats respectively on [[October 1]] and [[October 3]] of the same year.<ref name="jeproper date" /><ref name="jeamazon">{{RandomHouseOld|url=audio/catalog/display.pperl?isbn=0-375-41574-2|text=Star Wars: Agents of Chaos: Jedi Eclipse}}</ref> Narration for the audiobooks was provided by [[Anthony Heald]].<ref name="Heald 1">{{RandomHouseOld|url=audio/catalog/display.php?isbn=9780375417399|text=Star Wars: The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial}}</ref><ref name="Heald 2">{{RandomHouseOld|url=audio/catalog/display.php?isbn=9780375417511|text=Star Wars: The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse|nolive=1}}</ref> The covers of both novels were drawn by [[Artist/Legends|text=Artist]] [[Rick Berry]],<ref name="ht" /><ref name="je" /> whose initial cover of ''Hero's Trial'' featured a visibly different drawing of the character Elan than that which appeared on the final product.<ref name="arrive">{{SWArchive|url=eu/lit/novel/f20000720/index.html|text=Agents of Chaos Arrive}}</ref> For the books' Japanese releases, [[Tsuyoshi Nagano]] provided art for both covers.<ref name="jap1" /><ref name="jap2" />

==Release and reception==
{{Quote|On a personal note, conflicting fan reaction to my books kept me from knowing just what I was doing right, or wrong.|James Luceno, on the ''Agents of Chaos'' Duology|<ref name="Wrapping Up" />}}
James Luceno has indicated that ''Agents of Chaos'' received mixed fan reaction,<ref name="Wrapping Up" /> with some of the negative feedback stemming from his decision to separate Han and Leia Organa Solo.<ref name="Fictional Frontiers" /> ''Star Wars'' author [[Jason Fry]] has called ''Hero's Trial'' one of the best five installments of ''The New Jedi Order'', referring to Luceno's portrayal of Han Solo as a "moving portrait".<ref name="100 lessons" />

[[Sue Rostoni]], Executive Editor of [[Lucas Licensing]], has stated that all nineteen books of ''The New Jedi Order'' sold fairly equally, surprising the planning team with their consistently strong showing. Although she was unable to reveal specific sales figures,<ref name="sue">{{SWBoards|url=message.jspa?messageID=6393803#6393803|thread=Books, Comics, & Television VIPs|user=[[Sue Rostoni|Eeusu Estornii]]|date=2004-04-14|time=4:54 PM|quote=I can't give out sales figures. Sorry. For the NJO series, the books all sold fairly equally -- it didn't seem to matter who the author was.}}</ref> other novels of the series have had strong showings on the [[Wikipedia:New York Times Best Seller list|New York Times Best Seller list]].<ref name="vpnyt">{{WebCite|url=http://www.hawes.com/1999/1999-10-24.pdf|text=The New York Times Best Seller List – October 24, 1999|work=[http://www.hawes.com Hawes Publications]|archivedate=20170615134711}}</ref><ref name="vpnyt2">{{WebCite|url=http://www.hawes.com/1999/1999-11-14.pdf|text=The New York Times Best Seller List - November 14, 1999|work=[http://www.hawes.com Hawes Publications]|archivedate=20170615135025}}</ref><ref name="tufnyt">{{WebCite|url=http://www.hawes.com/2003/2003-11-23.pdf|text=The New York Times Best Seller List - November 23, 2003|work=[http://www.hawes.com Hawes Publications]|archivedate=20170615043605}}</ref><ref name="tufnyt2">{{WebCite|url=http://www.hawes.com/2003/2003-11-30.pdf|text=The New York Times Best Seller List - November 30, 2003|work=[http://www.hawes.com Hawes Publications]|archivedate=20190326032233}}</ref>

==Continuity==
{{Quote|I'm fond of the Jubilee Wheel as a reconstructed Marvel Wheel.|Daniel Wallace, on attempting to introduce a retcon regarding the ''Jubilee Wheel''|<ref name="Wallace">{{JCFCite|url=posts/24642988|board=Literature|thread=New Essential Guide to Droids|user=[[Daniel Wallace|Dan Wallace]]|date=2006-08-11|time=9:29 AM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200312130502/https://boards.theforce.net/threads/new-essential-guide-to-droids.21671810/page-16}}</ref>}}
[[File:Wheel.jpg|thumb|left|175px|The Wheel: Not the ''Jubilee Wheel'']]
Before signing on as a consultant during the planning of ''The New Jedi Order'', James Luceno had read many of the ''Star Wars'' novels published by Bantam-Spectra during the 1990s.<ref name="eucantinaluceno" /> The ''Agents of Chaos'' Duology features a large number of returning characters from several of those novels in large roles, minor roles, and cameos, and also makes reference to events and situations that occurred within their pages.<ref name="Continuity 1">For example, characters and organizations from [[1994]]'s ''[[The Courtship of Princess Leia]]'' that return in ''[[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse]]'' include the [[Hapes Consortium/Legends|Hapes Consortium]], [[Prince/Legends|Prince]] [[Isolder]], and [[Teneniel Djo]].</ref><ref name="Continuity 2">For example, characters from ''[[The Black Fleet Crisis]]'' Trilogy ([[1996]]-[[1997]]) who return in either of or both [[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial|''Hero's Trial'']] and [[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse|''Jedi Eclipse'']] include [[Turk Brand]], [[Ayddar Nylykerka]], [[Etahn A'baht]], [[Jowdrrl]], and [[Dryanta]].</ref><ref name="Continuity 3">For example, characters and situations from [[1995]]'s ''[[Star Wars: The Corellian Trilogy]]'' that return in ''[[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos II: Jedi Eclipse]]'' include [[Thrackan Sal-Solo]], [[Belindi Kalenda]], [[Showolter]], [[Marcha]], and the activation of [[Centerpoint Station]].</ref> ''Hero's Trial'' sees the return of several characters from Brian Daley's ''The Han Solo Adventures'',<ref name="Continuity 4">Characters from [[Star Wars: The Han Solo Adventures|''The Han Solo Adventures'']] who return or are referenced in ''[[The New Jedi Order: Agents of Chaos I: Hero's Trial]]'' include [[Roa]], [[Lwyll]], [[Big Bunji/Legends|Big Bunji]], [[Ploovo Two-For-One/Legends|Ploovo Two-For-One]], [[S. V. Skynx/Legends|S. V. Skynx]], and [[Gallandro/Legends|Gallandro]].</ref> as Luceno wanted to pay tribute to his deceased friend.<ref name="terrania" /><ref name="serving Sidious" />

Events from the duology were later referenced in other ''Star Wars'' media. ''[[The New Jedi Order Sourcebook]]'', published in [[2002]], includes a chapter titled "Agents of Chaos" that summarizes the events of both novels and gives [[Star Wars Roleplaying Game|roleplaying game]] statistics on characters such as Droma.<ref name="NJO Sourcebook">''[[The New Jedi Order Sourcebook]]''</ref> Droma and Vergere also received an entry in 2002's ''[[The New Essential Guide to Characters]]'',<ref name="negtc">''[[The New Essential Guide to Characters]]''</ref> and the events of ''Agents of Chaos'' were again summarized in [[2005]]'s ''[[The New Essential Chronology]]''<ref name="nec" /> and [[2012]]'s ''[[The Essential Reader's Companion]]''.<ref name="Reader's Companion" />

When Dan Wallace was writing [[2006]]'s ''[[The New Essential Guide to Droids]]'', he [[Retroactive continuity|implied]] that the ''Jubilee Wheel'', despite differences in structure and location, was the same space station as [[The Wheel/Legends|The Wheel]],<ref name="negtd">''[[The New Essential Guide to Droids]]''</ref><ref name="Wallace" /> a station first seen in the [[1978]] [[Star Wars comic books|comic book]] [[Star Wars (1977) 18|''Star Wars'' (1977) 18]].<ref name="strikes">[[Star Wars (1977) 18|''Star Wars'' (1977) 18]]</ref> However, the ''Jubilee Wheel'' is destroyed in ''Hero's Trial'', and The Wheel went on to appear in the [[2007]] comic book [[Legacy (2006) 9|''Legacy'' (2006) 9]], set [[137 ABY|more than 100 years]] later.<ref name="ht" /><ref name="Legacy 9">[[Legacy (2006) 9|''Legacy'' (2006) 9]]</ref> It was not until [[2013]] that authors [[Abel G. Peña]] and [[Rich Handley]], writing the article [[Viva Space Vegas! The History of the Marvelous Wheel]] for the [[StarWars.com]] blog, established that The Wheel and the ''Jubilee Wheel'' existed concurrently as competitors.<ref name="Viva Space Vegas">{{SW|url=news/viva-space-vegas-the-history-of-the-marvelous-wheel-part-1|serieslink=Viva Space Vegas! The History of the Marvelous Wheel|text=Part 1|nolive=1}}</ref>

In ''Hero's Trial'', Reck Desh meets with and receives information from someone dressed as a [[Kuati/Legends|Kuati]] [[telbun]].<ref name="ht" /> The individual was only confirmed to be Viqi Shesh in ''The New Jedi Order Sourcebook''.<ref name="NJO Sourcebook" />

==Legacy==
{{Quote|Because I had been with the series since its inception, and had the advantage of having been privy to just about every idea that had been tossed around, I emerged as the sort of default choice.|James Luceno, on being chosen to write ''The Unifying Force''|<ref name="Wrapping Up" />}}
[[File:Harrarheadshot.jpg|thumb|right|125px|Harrar served as a major character in the second half of ''The New Jedi Order''.]]
Plots and characters from ''Agents of Chaos'', such as Droma, the refugee problem, and Han Solo's estrangement from his family, went on to play large roles in the following entry of ''The New Jedi Order'', ''Balance Point''.<ref name="Balance Point" /> Some elements that were originally intended to carry on into the rest of the series were ultimately dropped, however.<ref name="terrania" /><ref name="Wrapping Up" /> ''Hero's Trial'' established a metaphysical conundrum on the part of C-3PO regarding [[Sentience/Legends|sentience]] and the fear of deactivation, realized in the wake of Chewbacca's death and the Yuuzhan Vong's harsh treatment of droids.<ref name="ht" /> However, this plotline was jettisoned when the series' planning team decided to focus more on Anakin Solo during the buildup to his death, which occurred in the series' ninth installment, ''[[The New Jedi Order: Star by Star]]''.<ref name="Wrapping Up" />

Luceno also hoped for Droma to remain as Solo's partner and co-pilot for several additional books, but when reader response indicated that fans wanted Solo's wife to join him in the ''Millennium Falcon'', Droma began to fade into the background.<ref name="terrania" /> Droma did resurface, however, in the series' seventeenth entry, ''[[The New Jedi Order: Force Heretic III: Reunion]]''. He appeared as the head of an organization called the [[Ryn Network]], which itself played a large role throughout the entire [[Force Heretic trilogy|''Force Heretic'' trilogy]].<ref name="fh1">''[[The New Jedi Order: Force Heretic I: Remnant]]''</ref><ref name="fh2">''[[The New Jedi Order: Force Heretic II: Refugee]]''</ref><ref name="fh3">''[[The New Jedi Order: Force Heretic III: Reunion]]''</ref> Harrar and Vergere, both creations of Luceno's, went on to play significant roles in the second half of ''The New Jedi Order''.<ref name="tuf">''[[The New Jedi Order: The Unifying Force]]''</ref><ref name="tfp">''[[The New Jedi Order: The Final Prophecy]]''</ref><ref name="traitor">''[[The New Jedi Order: Traitor]]''</ref><ref name="sbs">''[[The New Jedi Order: Star by Star]]''</ref>

Luceno was later chosen to write the nineteenth and final entry in ''The New Jedi Order'', [[2003]]'s ''[[The New Jedi Order: The Unifying Force]]''. Having been privy to nearly every idea that had been floated for the series, and knowledgable about the story arc and characters, he was a natural choice to the planning team to wrap up the series.<ref name="terrania" /><ref name="Wrapping Up" /> He has written five additional ''Star Wars'' novels,<ref name="cod">''[[Cloak of Deception]]''</ref><ref name="loe">''[[Labyrinth of Evil]]''</ref><ref name="dl">''[[Dark Lord: The Rise of Darth Vader]]''</ref><ref name="mf">[[Millennium Falcon (novel)|''Millennium Falcon'']]</ref><ref name="Plagueis">[[Darth Plagueis (novel)|''Darth Plagueis'']]</ref> one novella,<ref name="Saboteur">"[[Darth Maul: Saboteur]]"</ref> two short stories,<ref name="Restraint">{{StoryCite|story=Restraint|book=Darth Maul: Shadow Hunter}}</ref><ref name="End Game">{{StoryCite|story=End Game|book=Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace (novelization)|bformatted=''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'' novelization, 2012 edition}}</ref> and two reference books.<ref name="Inside the Worlds">''[[Inside the Worlds of Star Wars Trilogy]]''</ref><ref name="ROTS Visual Dictionary">''[[Star Wars: Revenge of the Sith: The Visual Dictionary]]''</ref> In 2007, he was identified by Sue Rostoni as an author that Lucasfilm Ltd. was not willing to lose.<ref name="notlose">{{Blog|eeusuestornii|9|Ballantine/Del Rey Fiction Schedule}}</ref>
